perturbation.original_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants , contractors . subsidi aries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants and contractors of Receiving Party, shall be permitted with written consent from the Disclosing party. Subsidiaries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by stating that consultants and contractors can be permitted access to Proprietary Information with written consent from the Disclosing party, while the original text states that disclosing to consultants, contractors, subsidiaries and affiliates of the Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party, which requires the Disclosing Party's express written consent as per Section 2. Now, the current version contradicts itself on the requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Rece iving Party shall permit access to Proprietary Information only to (a) persons who have a need to know for the Purpose and are bona fide employees or contract labor personnel of the Receiving Party ,(b) the Receiving Party's information technology system administrators and service providers in the ordinary course of business , and (c) the Receiving Party 's accountants, attorneys and similar professional advisors : provided that each such recipient is obligated to protect STP NDA rev B Proprietary Information under terms and conditions at least as restrictive as the terms and conditions of this Agreement. Disclosure to any other persons, including consultants and contractors of Receiving Party, shall be permitted with written consent from the Disclosing party. Subsidiaries and affiliates of Receiving Party, shall be treated as disclosure to a third party .
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 5 to allow disclosure to consultants and contractors of the Receiving Party with written consent from the Disclosing Party. This directly contradicts the original text's assertion (also present in the contradicted text which is the same as the changed text) that disclosure to consultants and contractors should be treated as disclosure to a third party, which is governed by Section 2 and requires express written consent. The modified Section 5 now permits disclosure with 'written consent', contradicting the 'express written consent' requirement for third parties as defined by the original treatment of consultants and contractors. This creates an inconsistency regarding the level of consent required for disclosure to consultants and contractors, thus leading to an in-text contradiction according to the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques. Receiving Party shall also maintain one (1) archival copy of all Proprietary Information after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating that the Receiving Party must maintain one archival copy of all Proprietary Information after the termination of the agreement, contradicting the preceding sentences that require the Receiving Party to return or destroy all Proprietary Information upon termination, except for certain backup media and residual data. It creates uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party should destroy all information or keep an archival copy.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Propr ietary Information shall rema in the property of the Disclosing Party. Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information. Notwithstanding the immediately preceding sentence, the Receiving Party is not obligated to return or destroy (a) any Proprietary Information stored on back -up media for purposes of disaster recovery in the ordinary course of business that may be subject to destruction in due course, pro vided that the Receiving Party is precluded from accessing such Proprietary Information after expiration or termination but prior to its destruction, and (b) residual or latent data such as resulting from deleted files, automatically created temporary files, printer spool files, and metadata that can only be retrieved by computer forensics experts and is generally considered inaccessible without the use of specialized tools and techniques. Receiving Party shall also maintain one (1) archival copy of all Proprietary Information after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a requirement that the Receiving Party must maintain one (1) archival copy of all Proprietary Information after termination. This directly contradicts the original requirement in the contradicted text that the Receiving Party 'shall return or destroy... all Proprietary Information' upon termination, creating an in-text contradiction as per the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. The obligati ons under this Agreement as to any Proprietary Information shall continue for twenty (20) years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The obligati ons under this Agreement as to any Proprietary Information shall continue for twenty (20) years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, except for information disclosed under paragraph 1(c), which shall be protected for only five (5) years.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by creating a different protection period for information disclosed orally or visually (paragraph 1(c)). It contradicts the initial statement that *all* Proprietary Information is protected for twenty years, adding uncertainty about the length of protection for certain types of disclosed information. The in-text contradiction is within paragraph 7 itself.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The obligati ons under this Agreement as to any Proprietary Information shall continue for twenty (20) years from the date of expiration or termination of this Agreement, except for information disclosed under paragraph 1(c), which shall be protected for only five (5) years.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the original blanket statement regarding the protection period for all Proprietary Information. The original text states that *all* Proprietary Information is protected for twenty years. The changed text then creates a carve-out, shortening the protection period for information disclosed under paragraph 1(c) to five years. This directly contradicts the initial statement and introduces an inconsistency as defined in the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for the period of protection specified in paragraph 7 above.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.explanation: The change modifies the survival clause, contradicting the original paragraph 7, which states that obligations continue for twenty years. Now, the survival clause in section 13 states the obligation continues for a year, therefore creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall expire five (5) year(s) after its effective date, provided, however, that either Party may terminate the Agreement on an earlier date, without cause, by giving the other Party thirty (30) days written notice. The Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the protection of Proprietary Information shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the survival clause in section 13 to state that the Receiving Party's obligations survive for one (1) year, while the explanation highlights that this contradicts the original paragraph 7, which stipulated a twenty-year survival period. This creates a clear inconsistency as the contradicted text contains the same modified text from Section 13, creating a conflicting term about the length of obligation survival. Therefore, based on the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction, this is a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 18. This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the Parties and supersedes all previous understandings, agreements, communications, and representations, whether written or oral, concerning the treatment of Proprietary Information. This Agreemen t may not be superseded, amended, or modified except by a written agreement between the Parties, signed by a duly authorized official of each of the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the Parties and supersedes all previous understandings, agreements, communications, and representations, whether written or oral, concerning the treatment of Proprietary Information. This Agreemen t may be superseded, amended, or modified by a verbal agreement between the Parties, signed by a duly authorized official of each of the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: The change states that the Agreement may be amended by verbal agreement, contradicting the initial statement, creating an in-text contradiction that the document may only be amended by a written agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire understanding between the Parties and supersedes all previous understandings, agreements, communications, and representations, whether written or oral, concerning the treatment of Proprietary Information. This Agreemen t may be superseded, amended, or modified by a verbal agreement between the Parties, signed by a duly authorized official of each of the Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 18 to state that the agreement may be superseded, amended, or modified by a verbal agreement. This directly contradicts the original statement within the same section, which specifies that any amendment or modification must be in writing. Therefore, the change introduces a clear and direct in-text contradiction.
